author: @NazimHosssain
text:
I don’t believe it’s necessary to upgrade to the S26 Ultra if you already own the S23 Ultra. This year’s update does not bring anything truly exciting. There are a few interesting additions, such as the privacy display and horizon video feature, which are impressive, but beyond that, the improvements feel minor. The camera has been slightly enhanced, but not to a level that justifies a full upgrade.
As an Android user, I still feel that Samsung is missing some practical features that should be included in future models. An IR blaster would be useful, faster charging technology would be welcome, and a larger battery capacity or improved battery efficiency would make a real difference. The display is already very good, but there is still room for refinement.
Overall, it is a solid phone, but nothing particularly special this year. For those who already own a device in the S Ultra lineup, upgrading does not seem necessary.

author: @atif32111
text:
Sticking with the 5000mAh battery was a smart and practical decision. Instead of taking the easy route like some brands that simply increase battery capacity, it’s good to see a focus on real optimization and efficiency. Charging with 45w was also more than enough.
The Privacy Display is a nice addition, but it feels like too much hype was created around it just to make it the headline feature.
When comparing the S25 Ultra and S26 Ultra, the difference isn’t significant enough to feel like a true generational leap. It’s more of a refinement than a day-and-night upgrade, so existing users may not find a strong reason to switch.
That said, the new 10-bit display is a welcome improvement. Better color depth along with more efficient power consumption makes it a meaningful enhancement and definitely something worth appreciating.
At this point, raw performance has become somewhat irrelevant for everyday use. Most users spend their time on social media, browsing, messaging, and other routine tasks where current flagship hardware is already more than powerful enough. Instead of chasing higher benchmark scores every year, the real focus should be on better thermal management, sustained performance, and long-term system stability. A cooler, more consistent device in daily use would deliver far more practical value than marginal gains in peak performance numbers.
More rounded corners feel like a step away from its signature identity. It’s disappointing to see it move away from the bold, Ultra-style design—similar to the Note series—and become a more ordinary-looking phone, losing the distinctive Ultra character that once set it apart.
The sharper edges and boxy frame weren’t just about aesthetics—they gave the device a sense of purpose, professionalism, and premium authority. That design language made it feel like a productivity powerhouse rather than just another flagship. With the softer curves, it starts to blend into the crowd, reducing the visual and emotional distinction that made the Ultra line special.
Part of the appeal of the Ultra series was that it didn’t try to please everyone—it catered to users who wanted something bold, functional, and unmistakably different. Moving toward a safer, more mainstream design risks diluting that identity. When a flagship loses its strong visual personality, it also loses some of the excitement and pride of ownership that comes with carrying a device that stands out.
If this trend continues, the Ultra may still be powerful internally, but externally it risks feeling less iconic—more like just another premium phone, rather than the statement device it was meant to be.
Overall, the device brings thoughtful refinements, but the pricing and incremental upgrades make the value proposition harder to justify for current Ultra users.

Hey everyone,

I have a Samsung S10 that I bought brand new back in 2019 — one of the first around here. The phone still works fine and everything is functional, but honestly I feel like it’s time for an upgrade. I used to follow phones closely and understood all the tech, but the last few years I’ve had zero time to keep up, so I could use some advice.
What would you recommend and why?

What matters to me:

-great camera
-good zoom

-something that will last as long as my S10

Is it worth going for the S26 Ultra 1TB / 16GB, or is there a better option right now?

Thanks in advance!! 👋

author: @spyplane8
text:
WHAT IS IT GOING TO TAKE TO GET PEOPLE TO STOP BUYING SAMSUNG, GOOGLE AND APPLE!!   THESE COMPANIES ARE BLATANTLY EXPLOITING CONSUMERS AND CONSUMERS SEEM TO REALLY LOVE BEING EXPLOITED!! 

Also there are very good tempered glass privacy screen protectors!   They look very high quality and work amazing!   This hardware privacy screen also dimms the display identically to the tempered glass privacy screen protectors!    THIS IS NOT A REASON TO BUY A PHONE WHICH IS EXACTLY WHAT SAMSUNG IS GOING TO ATTEMPT TO CLAIM!! 

Samsung made a massive deal around the anti-reflective screen coating that is no longer on the ULTRA!    The biggest reason in my opinion is because that coating failed badly over time!   The only reason I purchased the s25 ultra from the s24 ultra was because of the coating failing!   Guess what?   It's failing again!!  Anyone that uses their phone for work is constantly touching in specific spots on the display.  Well those places begin to have a horrible dirty look to them which is where that coating begins to break down!   My S25 ultra looks exactly the same!  I take amazing care of my devices and the only way you can prevent the coating from failing is to put a screen protector on the phone which eliminates the anti reflective properties!   

The gallery already had the option to REMASTER photos!  Simply touch a photo in the gallery and click the REMASTER option.  

I have been doing my best to try and make everyone understand how pathetic Samsung has become! First, I own all Samsung appliances in my home: refrigerator, stove, washer, dryer, and dishwasher. The only reason I own the S25 Ultra is because the S24 Ultra had the anti-reflective screen coating failing (which the S25 Ultra is doing the exact same thing). My point is, I'm not someone bashing Samsung; I am someone that was very loyal to the brand. Also, to put it in perspective, I own an IT/technology company and have been working with technology for 27 years at this point.  Mobile devices are something that end users rely on every day.  It's something that I run my entire business on for the most part.  My customers will not be getting recommended any devices from Samsung, Google, or Apple ever again. If we have to, we'll import.  

What is actually happening at Samsung? 
I'll tell you. 

People need to understand that Samsung changed leadership. The new president of Mobile technology is Lee Jae-yong.  Samsung made an internal decision to move away from being a hardware brand and more towards a "lifestyle brand." That simply means trying to become Apple.   With that said, Samsung decided the best course of action was to begin utilizing as much of the same internal hardware as possible year over year in order to increase profit, and the entire focus is on the ecosystem.  This is exactly what anyone that said they "have" to upgrade to Samsung's S26 Ultra because they have a Samsung tablet has fallen for.  

Samsung's actual device specifications: 

The reality is, Samsung has not upgraded camera hardware in any significant way since the S23 Ultra, which has a main sensor called the Isocell HP2. It's true Samsung does create some of the best camera sensors in the world (HP9, HP5, or a custom version of the HP9 called the HPB). Samsung does not utilize any of their top-of-the-line sensors in any of their devices.  Samsung provides those top-of-the-line sensors—the HP9, HPB, and HP5—to their global competition: Honor, Oppo, Xiaomi, Vivo. That's correct, while Samsung utilizes their inexpensive, dated, very small HP2 in their devices, they are assisting their global competition to make generational leaps in camera performance.   These global brands also utilize the latest in silicon-carbon battery technology, which is far superior technology along with these devices having far superior charging speeds.

Samsung, Google, and Apple have completely ignored silicon-carbon battery tech.   There is no excuse at this point, considering the fact that silicon-carbon has now been proven superior and reliable.   The only true reason that Samsung, Google, and Apple have not changed is profit and they have large stock piles of the identical battery cells they have utilizes for years! Ask yourself, why wouldn't Apple or Google realize there's a huge opportunity here to jump to silicon-carbon before Samsung?There's a speculation on my part, but the only answer is most likely that those three companies make synchronized decisions on upgrades. If you notice, none of these brands ever make big jumps in hardware over their competitors in the United States.  Most likely, they are talking, and my guess is whenever these companies move to silicon carbon, they will do it simultaneously so they all retain their same portion of the market.  Like I said, if Google or Apple wanted to really take advantage of this, one of them would have jumped on silicon carbon and left the others in the dust.  Any business owner would realize they could truly benefit by doing so if they hadn't made some type of agreement. 



The HP2 sensor that Samsung continues using is very small and struggles badly with capturing pictures without perfect lighting. This sensor was first utilized in the S23. This mediocre sensor is also utilized in the S24 Ultra, S25 Ultra, and now the "NEW"🤣 S26 Ultra.  This is exactly what Samsung meant when they decided to become a lifestyle brand and work to utilize as much hardware year over year.   

No matter how hard people try and spin it, the S23 Ultra, S24 Ultra, S25 Ultra, and the S26 Ultra are nearly all identical devices. The only difference in hardware that really makes a difference is the next-generation processor that they utilize from Qualcomm (The Snapdragon). The devices all use identical battery cells.  The S25 Ultra received a different ultrawide sensor, which is Samsung's truly mediocre  JN3 50-megapixel. There was only a slight improvement in quality to a sensor that is not used that often.  

People don't realize why capitalism works so well! People have to understand that their money holds an extremely large amount of power. When a company betrays you and stops innovating, you can buy another product.  The previous company that you purchased from either changes and realizes what they've done or they decline.  The biggest problem is people continue buying products from companies when they completely abandon innovation. Samsung, Google, and Apple all continue thriving because people simply buy their products and accept what they're creating.  If everyone stopped buying Samsung, Samsung would revert to creating innovative, top-of-the-line hardware!  At this point, the internal decision by their new president has been very successful; Samsung's profit margin has skyrocketed! This has allowed Samsung to completely abandon the power users that were basically responsible for putting Samsung's mobile devices on the map.  

After years of buying Samsung and being truly excited for their products, I will never buy another Samsung product again. 

When I saw that Vivo was bringing the X300 Ultra and Oppo the X9 Ultra to the global market, that was the first time my stomach jumped and I was excited for a mobile device in a decade!Does anyone remember those days when you were excitedly refreshing a web page or waiting for a release so you could get your hands on a new mobile device? It's crazy that people just went along with these mobile device manufacturers suddenly ending innovation in exchange for exploiting as much profit for as little innovation as possible. 
Let's hope people realize the internal privacy display from Samsung isn't good enough!  My guess is people will flock to buy the S26 Ultra, which means my dollars will be permanently used elsewhere!

author: @EltecnicoWD
text:
Disappointed. TLDR. As a Samsung loyalist since 2012, it is frustrating how these reviews feel like sanitized PR. Even when guys like Mrwhosetheboss or MKBHD mention the negatives, they fly right past them without the emphasis they deserve. Samsung is charging a $1,300 premium while arguably delivering a regression in hardware.

It’s wild that they prioritized making the phone 0.3mm thinner instead of using new silicon-carbon battery tech or increasing capacity. Like Mrwhosetheboss said, that 5,000mAh tank is a ‘family heirloom’ at this point. Worse, the new Privacy Display actually hurts the viewing angles and brightness for the user, and the battery longevity has dropped to 1,200 cycles. We are also glossing over the S Pen being a 'dumb' stylus for the second year in a row that now only fits into the slot one specific way.

Even with MKBHD calling out the 'price creep' and AI 'slop,' these points are buried under praise. We shouldn't be trading core hardware functionality and longevity for a thinner design and software gimmicks. I want the Ultra to actually be Ultra again.

author: @Telecuhubby
text: I know this is what channels like this are for and what a lot of us geek out over but for once can we show real world applications and displays in a review? For example, I think 95% of the population cannot explain what the difference is between a snapdragon elite 4 or elite 5 other than surface level "it's faster and more battery efficient". This display review is sick. It's a simple explanation but it's a revolutionary feature . I also think back to examples of the one plus phones going with silicon batteries or how life changing it is to have 80watt charging. Again ,simple explanation but "your phone will charge 50% in 10-15mins"is life changing. I can't tell you how little I think about charging since moving to the one plus phones and each year they last longer and charge faster. This is what I mean by practical features and their explanation. I don't want to know that new phones have a bigger aperture to let more light in, I want to see 2 photos side by side displaying the difference and wowing my eyes into seeing that night and day feature that will make me considering selling or trading in my current phone to run out and buy a new one. On that note, the privacy display is nice but if I'm being real I only notice stylistic changes in camera photos these days. Like iPhones warm temp, pixel punchiness, Samsung contrast,etc. Actually quality is the same to me across the board with minor exceptions of "this one is better at xzoom or this one has less noise at night by a hair".

author: @youssefayman6565
text:
The question remains: why would someone choose the S26 Ultra over the Oppo Find X9 Ultra, the iPhone 17 Pro Max, or any other current flagship? Personally, I'd point to the S24 Ultra as the smarter buy. Yes, the S26 Ultra is the latest and greatest, and its AI features are impressive.. but most of those capabilities will likely roll out to older devices through software updates anyway. When you factor in the price difference and overall value, the S24 Ultra still holds its own, with guaranteed updates running through nearly 2030.
On top of that, the S26 Ultra ships with a 5,000 mAh battery.. the same capacity as my old S22 Ultra. That's actually one of the main reasons I switched to the Oppo Find X9 Pro.
 I just don't see the S26 Ultra as a meaningful leap forward, especially for the price. It's newer, sure, and it brings some fresh capabilities, but from a value for money perspective, why upgrade to a flagship from early 2025 when a device from early 2023 still keeps pace? The AI features are impressive, but the reality is that the vast majority of them are available on most modern devices already.

author: @LittleLily_
text: With the way that the screen hardware for the privacy display feature works, it does mean the off-axis brightness of the screen is compromised compared to a regular screen unfortunately. That's cause they basically replaced half the pixels on the screen with pixels that have narrow viewing angles, so under normal use both sets of pixels are active, and when the privacy feature is enabled only the pixels with narrow viewing angles are active. This means that even with the feature disabled, you end up with the brightness decreasing off-axis (since only the regular pixels will be visible from that angle), and with the feature enabled you end up with less brightness in all cases (even front on) because half the pixels are completely off. So basically if you don't plan on using that feature, the screen is objectively worse than a screen without support for that feature.

author: @SaberSlayer88
text:
I hate how I guessed Samsung would revert back to aluminum construction from stainless steel and titanium, following step for step Apples decision. It was at first the one time both companies opted to make their product more expensive to manufacture without initially raising the price to consumers to give us a better product. Titanium in a phone frame is incredible. Basically as light weight as aluminum and significantly more durable. Not only is the anodized finish seriously scratch resistant in real world scenarios (dirt and sand in between the phone and case) but its much less prone to deformation from a drop outside of a case. If you drop an aluminum framed phone on the ground from even 3 feet it will literally leave a dent let alone the heavy gouging and removal of anodized finish. You dent that aluminum right where the frame meets the screen and you will never get the screen to lay down correctly after you replace it (because its guaranteed to break under this drop). 

If you follow Jerryrig, you know that almost all modern phones do not fail the bend test. I'll remind the reader that it was the aluminum iPhone 6 that famously had the bend gate scandal. They remedied the issue with the iPhone 7 for sure, however it does help illustrate how easily the material can be deformed. I see this as an egregious backpedaling from both brands, while charging even more for phones that are fundamentally cheaper to manufacture than last years model. Id get the previous gen models if you're in the market for that reason alone.

author: @UnknownbutKnow-b4h
text:
MAFIA OF PAKISTAN 

              ARMY CHEIF  ASIM MUNIR 

The Pakistan Army does not protect the country; it controls the country. For many years, it has ruled Pakistan’s politics, media, and courts while serving Western interests. What the state calls “terrorism” is often a false story. Groups like the Taliban are used as tools by the army to justify violence, operations, and fear. This story is mainly sold to the Punjabi people so they stay loyal to the army and its uniform, while the real harm is done to ordinary Pakistanis.

When Imran Khan refused to follow Western orders—especially when he went to Russia despite clear pressure not to—the situation changed. Western countries turned against him, and General Qamar Javed Bajwa supported a no-confidence move to remove his government. Later, when the people again showed support for Imran Khan in the February 8, 2024 elections, the army ignored the public vote and reversed the results. With Western approval, General Asim Munir took control and now runs the country using hidden martial-law tactics.

Imran Khan’s biggest mistake, in their eyes, was that he did not surrender. He exposed how the army controls the media, manages the courts, pressures judges, and crushes political opponents. The violence on November 26—when peaceful PTI protesters were killed at D-Chowk, Sahiwal, and Muridke—proved one thing clearly: the real terror in Pakistan is not from militants, but from the state itself. Democracy is only a show, justice is controlled, media is silenced, and power is kept by force, not by the will of the people.

text: I got the S25 in December and feared that maybe I should have waited as the S26 might be a big leap forward. So happy I'm part of the Samsung club with a 'if it ain't broke don't fix it' mentality, one of the best phones on the market, so why change it.

author: @adityashah3751
text:
Marques, for the S26 Ultra review, please don't just grade it on a curve against the S25 Ultra or the iPhone 17. The US smartphone bubble is blinding us to what's happening globally. Yes, bumping the S26 Ultra's main aperture to f/1.4 is a nice incremental upgrade, but the Oppo Find X9 Pro and Vivo X300 Pro are completely resetting the hardware standard with massive 1/1.4-inch 200MP periscope telephotos and massive primary sensors. 

Can we get a critical review of whether Samsung's aging hardware and heavy computational pipeline can still genuinely compete with the sheer optical physics of the global industry leaders? 

That's the shootout we actually need!

author: @japanviaharis
text:
The prvacy display is enabled by a hardware tech they called BLACK MATRIX.
It is a physical filter on half of the pixels and when it's turned on, it narrows the light emission only to front direction.
When turned off the other half of the pixels are also activated and emit light in every direction.
That's why when you turn on the filter, the display become a little dimmer.
But the fact it works very seemlessly with the display animation is very sick.

author: @noonenoesbutme
text:
I work for a small R&D team in Tech as the lead mechanical engineer. Because of the AI boom, and the big AI companies purchasing like 60-75% if the world RAM fab for 2026 - RAM gas shot through the roof.

A computer I'm designing had the price of RAM (bare chips for industrial use, NOT consumer sticks) jump from $200 last October to now $900.

The fact that thes phones only increased by $100, is a miracle and I guarantee you that Samsung is taking a loss on year-over-year profit by only increasing the price of their flagship phone by $100. 

The PlayStation 6 and The steam console are both delayed permanently until the price of RAM becomes reasonable. 

Memory is going to be very expensive for the next couple years, until new fabs enter the market.

Everything from your iPhone, to a budget PC, to an engineering grade Pro level computer - it's all going up in price significantly until the planet can increase fabrication output of memory.

author: @DylanGoldbyPhotographer
text:
The changes here make absolute sense from a Korean perspective and I can see these phones, especially the Ultra being incredibly popular here in Korea. People are obsessed, sometimes to a fault, with personal privacy and this is one more step towards that in a place where everyone is watching everyone all the time. Business meetings, browsing on the train, etc. will all see a massive spike in the use of this. 

On top of that, the government is pushing AI in every way possible to try and make Korea the world leader. You're exactly right with your point of throw things at the wall and see what sticks. That's pretty much how business in general works here and Samsung is no exception. They've got to justify their tax incentives somehow!

author: @PlanetVenuz
text: 3:40 The reason for the screen dimming is caused by the hardware in the screen. The technology used involves adding a sort of "lens" on some of the pixels while keeping others normal. This lens essentially narrows the light out of the pixels to reduce the viewable angle. When the privacy screen is enabled, Only these pixels are on. When it is off, All of the pixels are on. It's kinda unavoidable with this technology since it has to limit the light that is output

text:
Why is the battery worse for the S26/Ultra if other brands like Motorola and One Plus have phones in the last year that have larger batteries? It's not EU regulations dictating phone battery size in the US, it's the US knowing that when 59% have iOS devices and 40% or so have Android, that at the end of the day It's a South Korean company knowing they can sell less for more because America is far too consumerist for it's own good, and us not having regulatory standards that push these companies to compete on margins while their profits keep expanding dooms us. 

I agree with everything else you said, but you can get Chinese phones with global ROMs that have 7,000+ milliamp hours easily these days. And there's plenty of other brands as well that are optimizing battery tech, Samsung doesn't want to yet because they value their profits, regardless of EU regulations that are good sometimes and bad others like anything.

author: @Mikkel111
text:
I tried a privacy protector about 6 years ago. But it never really got dark enough when the screen was above 40% brightness, and it tinted the entire screen yellow. Plus, it reduced the sensitivity of the screen.
Last year, I installed a privacy protector again, and I'm impressed with how much better it performs. It gets unreadably dark as long as you're below 80% brightness and doesn't tint the screen or noticeably reduce sensitivity.
So if this built-in (I assume polarizer) tech can improve and make it onto other phones, I'd be really interested. Because it is still annoying trying to show your phone to someone else and having to get both of you in the sweetspot.

author: @Osprey1994
text:
My last Dbrand case separated twice and needed to be replaced.  I didn't ask for a replacement after the second time, I just bought a UAG case which pasted for the duration of my time with that phone.  

Personally I am still on the S23+ and until I reach the point where it's dying faster than is reasonable (already replaces the battery once last year).  Then I don't know if I will be upgrading.  I also have thr option to switching to another S23+ that is lightly used which may net me another few years.  As it stands, phones within the past 4ish years, have been so powerful that it doesn't really feel like upgrading is as much of a temptation.  In the past, I felt a tangible improvement from phone to phone, whether it was the screen, speakers, battery, storage, processing speed, etc.  My past bump from S21+ to S23+ wasn't as noticeable as previous upgrades, and my experience with newer phones like the Google Pixel 10 Pro don't strike me as huge upgrades.

author: @bitw00f
text:
Who the hell cares about all this AI nonsense?

The Bluetooth is still missing from the s-pen.
The materials for the frame got downgraded back to Aluminum, although this one I can tolerate due to its superior weight and heat conductivity.
The battery capacity stayed the same, no new battery tech or larger battery cells because phones must be paper thin for some reason.
The 3x telephoto camera is still 10 megapixels with the same awful optics. I hope they didn't shrink the sensor like the rumors specified (they did >_>).
The new optics may bring an improvement to the main camera and the 5x telephoto, but an even larger camera bump completely negates any thinness the rest of the frame may have.
Main camera hardware is almost unchanged for like the 4-th year in a row. Only optics improvements, which is good but not enough.
The "revolutionary" processor is at best on the same level with the new Snapdragon.
Still no seamless display without camera cutout.
Still no built-in magnets for proper wireless charging.

Am I missing something, or is "AI" (which most people don't use) and privacy screen the only real improvement this year?
Aren't AI features going to come to older models anyway? So it is pretty much just the privacy screen...

author: @SalvadorASM
text: Re: the camera specs, going from f/1.7->1.4 and f/3.4->2.9 (not 2.4 that you mentioned) is actually pretty significant. The f/1.4 main camera captures 47% more light, and the f/2.9 telephoto captures 37% more. That's because aperture area scales with the square of the f-stop ratio.  This plus the AI-enhanced ISP should make low light pics much better.

text: As you asked, I would like to see in your reviews more commentary about locked bootloaders and the possibility of rooting. It is a niche thing, but it is important about freedom and ownership. Now you can't, and for many of us in that niche, it was the difference between choosing this brand and operating system vs. the competition. Now that's gone, I won't buy any more devices from them, nor will I recommend them either. They won't care, of course, but if more people were aware of this stuff, it could be a decisive thing to analyze when deciding to buy or not a certain brand or model.

author: @FattusCat
text: I’m disappointed, this was going to be my next upgrade from my S24 Ultra but there just isn’t enough on offer. Privacy has never been a big thing for me, sure it’s cool and will probably catch on but it’s not a selling point for me. My phone is also Titanium and while I appreciate aluminium is better for cooling, it’s also less durable. The anodised colours can easily scratch revealing the silver aluminium shining through, I know this because I have had previous aluminium phones. By comparison, my gold titanium S24U has firstly never scratched (a first for me) and even if it did, it wouldn’t be noticeable. I wanted bigger innovations and upgrades to the camera, yes there have been improvements and I’ve watched MrWhosTheBosses video and there is a clear improvement, especially on the low light side of things and the selfie cam…but not enough for me to spend the £1500+ for the 1TB version I’d be replacing mine with. But the biggest turn off was the lack of pre-order deals. In the UK they are only doubling the 256gb for free and giving you £30 with a pre order. In previous years they doubled all storage for free in pre order and also gave you a watch and a Disney+ subscription for the year. In all it was a good few hundreds worth of freebies. I will either wait for the S27U and hope for better battery, charging, magnets, cameras or I’ll take a look and switching it up completely with the rumoured Fold 8 Wide. As for software, we all get that eventually so isn’t a reason to upgrade IMO.

author: @mouseman225
text: The beginning of the end was when Samsung launched their "100x Space Zoom" which, to no one's surprise, was just digitally enhanced/faked images of the moon. One guy put a purposely blurry picture of the moon on his computer monitor, took a picture of that with "space zoom" and Samsung produced a perfectly clean, crisp, and detailed photo of the moon from a subject that had none of the detail the camera produced. How can we trust photo and video when phones do that kind of stuff without telling us? How many pictures have details added that never existed in reality? How many pictures of my daughter have been altered with smoothing and "beautifying" effects? I don't want to remember Samsungs version of my daughter. I want to remember my actual daughter, her real, less-than-perfect self. Time to buy an old point and shoot from before these digital "enhancements" were injected into our technology.

"throwing a bunch of AI stuff out there to see what will stick and what people will like..."

Dear Samsung,

Throwing in the latest Snapdragon and changing the radius of the curves on the body don't count as innovation btw, just for the record
